skill.name: building-multiagent-systems
skill.description: Architecture guidance for designing multi-agent AI systems with orchestration, delegation, tool coordination, lifecycle cleanup, cost tracking, and production safety controls.
skill.summary: Design coordinated AI agent systems with practical architecture patterns and safety guidance.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found Markdown architecture guidance and TypeScript-style examples rather than executable scripts or malicious code. The findings are mainly false positives from inline code, schema examples, and illustrative agent snippets. Publish with a warning because the skill discusses shell-capable agents, file access, and self-modifying workflows that require strict permissions in real implementations.

medium_findings.title: Risky Agent Capability Guidance Requires Permission Controls
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends architectures where agents may coordinate file I/O, shell commands, database access, and self-modifying code workflows. This is not malicious because it appears as instructional Markdown, but real implementations must restrict permissions, validate tool calls, and avoid unsafe rollback commands on user work.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The concern is semantic: the cited text explicitly discusses shell execution permissions and self-modification examples. Confidence is medium because the content is documentation, not executable code.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged Markdown backticks and TypeScript examples as Ruby or shell execution. Review found these are inline references, diagrams, and illustrative snippets, with no executable files or package hooks in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown prose or fenced examples and do not execute during skill loading. The repository contains only Markdown files and the report file.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive File and Weak Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high and medium alerts for weak cryptography, database files, and certificate or key files resolve to generic words such as schema keys, TypeScript fields, IDs, and database example methods. No evidence found of embedded secrets, real key material, or weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found example data structures and method names, not secret files or cryptographic code. The scanner categories do not match the surrounding context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Instructional Examples Include Shell-Enabled Agent Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples where a parent agent has shell execution permission and sub-agents receive narrower scopes. This is useful guidance but dangerous if copied without sandboxing, approval gates, and command allowlists.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell permission appears directly in the example, but the surrounding text says child agents cannot escalate privileges. Risk depends on how a user implements the pattern.

content.value_statement: Multi-agent projects often fail through unclear delegation, unsafe tools, and uncontrolled costs. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured patterns for coordinated agent architectures.
actual_capabilities: Guides discovery questions before selecting a multi-agent architecture.
Explains a four-layer stack for reasoning, orchestration, tools, and adapters.
Compares fan-out, pipeline, delegation, queue, map-reduce, peer, and MAKER patterns.
Provides guidance for permission inheritance, resource locks, rate limits, and caching.
Describes production controls for cleanup, checkpoints, cost tracking, and orphan detection.
limitations: Provides architecture guidance, not a runnable multi-agent framework.
Requires users to adapt examples to their language, model provider, and runtime.
Does not verify correctness of deployed agent systems automatically.
High-risk tool access still needs sandboxing, approval flows, and security review.

use_cases.title: Design Review Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Plan specialist agents for security, performance, tests, and style with clear aggregation rules.

use_cases.title: Build Production Orchestrators
use_cases.description: Define lifecycle, checkpointing, cost tracking, and failure handling before implementation.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Agent Coordination Patterns
use_cases.description: Choose between fan-out, pipelines, delegation, queues, map-reduce, councils, or MAKER workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Select a Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new agent workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a multi-agent coordination pattern for this task. Ask the required discovery questions first, then recommend one pattern with trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: Draft an Agent Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate design for a product feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a multi-agent architecture using the four-layer stack. Include agent roles, tool scopes, state handling, and cleanup behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Harden an Existing System
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of a working implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this multi-agent design for production risks. Focus on orphan detection, cost tracking, checkpointing, rate limits, and permission inheritance.

prompt_templates.title: Model a Zero-Error Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the MAKER pattern to this high-risk workflow. Define decomposition depth, voting thresholds, validation gates, retry rules, and expected cost.

output_examples.input: Design agents for reviewing a large pull request.
output_examples.output: A fan-out plan with four reviewer agents, scoped read-only tools, timeouts, partial-failure handling, cost aggregation, and a final synthesis step.

output_examples.input: Harden a multi-agent data processing pipeline.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering checkpoints between stages, shared rate limits, stale task release, resource locks, and cascading stop behavior.

output_examples.input: Choose a pattern for thousands of independent tasks.
output_examples.output: A work-stealing queue recommendation with retry policy, concurrency limits, task claiming rules, and monitoring requirements.

best_practices: Ask the discovery questions before designing roles, tools, or orchestration.
Give sub-agents the smallest practical permission set and explicit timeouts.
Add checkpoints, cost accounting, and cascading cleanup before production use.
anti_patterns: Spawning unlimited agents without batching, budgets, or lifecycle cleanup.
Giving child agents broad shell or write access without approval controls.
Mixing LLM reasoning inside deterministic tool adapters.

faq.question: Does this skill provide runnable code?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance and examples that users adapt to their own stack.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is structured for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Can it help with production readiness?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers cleanup, checkpointing, cost tracking, orphan detection, and coordination primitives.

faq.question: Does it require a specific programming language?
faq.answer: No. The concepts are language-neutral, although examples use TypeScript-style pseudocode.

faq.question: Is it safe for agents with shell access?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill discusses shell-capable agents, so implementations need sandboxing and approval gates.

faq.question: When should I use the MAKER pattern?
faq.answer: Use it for high-assurance workflows where subtasks can be decomposed, validated, and voted on.
